> **(c) Use of deadly force by a law enforcement officer.** When, in fact, the actor is a law enforcement officer who uses or attempts to use deadly force, a factfinder shall consider all of the following when determining whether the actor satisfies the requirements of the defense:
>
> (1) The law enforcement officer's training and experience;
>
> (2) Whether the complainant:
>
> (A) Appeared to possess, either on their person or in a location where it is readily available, a dangerous weapon; and
>
> (B) Was afforded an opportunity to comply with an order to surrender any suspected dangerous weapons;
>
> (3) Whether the law enforcement officer engaged in de-escalation measures, including taking cover, waiting for back-up, trying to calm the complainant, or using non-deadly force;
>
> (4) Whether any conduct by the law enforcement officer increased the risk of a confrontation resulting in deadly force being used; and
>
> (5) Whether the law enforcement officer made all reasonable efforts to prevent a loss of a life, including abandoning efforts to apprehend the complainant.

#### MPC Counterpart

MPC Section 3.04 provides, in relevant part:

> **(1)** ... the use of force upon or toward another person is justifiable when the actor believes that such force is immediately necessary for the purpose of protecting himself against the use of unlawful force by such other person on the present occasion.
>
> **(2)(b)** The use of deadly force is not justifiable under this Section unless the actor believes that such force is necessary to protect himself against death, serious bodily injury, kidnapping or sexual intercourse compelled by force or threat ...
>
> **(2)(b)(ii)** [The actor is not obliged to retreat] ... in his dwelling or place of work, unless he was the initial aggressor ...

The MPC imposes a duty to retreat before using deadly force when the actor knows they can do so with complete safety, except in the actor's dwelling or place of work. MPC Section 3.07, in a separate provision, addresses use of force by law enforcement officers.

#### Key Differences

**No Duty to Retreat.** The most prominent divergence between these provisions concerns retreat. The MPC requires an actor to retreat before using deadly force if they know they can do so with "complete safety," subject to a dwelling exception. The RCC imposes no duty to retreat whatsoever, consistent with longstanding D.C. case law. Under the RCC, the question is solely whether the use of force was "necessary in degree" to protect against the threat, and the factfinder evaluates necessity without a categorical obligation to withdraw. This reflects the traditional D.C. position that a person lawfully present in any location need not yield ground to an aggressor.

**Expanded Castle Doctrine.** Both codes recognize heightened protection within the dwelling, but they define the scope of that protection differently. Under the MPC, the dwelling exception merely eliminates the duty to retreat; deadly force remains justified only against death, serious bodily injury, kidnapping, or compelled sexual intercourse. The RCC goes further: within the actor's "individual dwelling unit," deadly force is permissible when the actor reasonably believes they face imminent danger of mere "bodily injury" or "sexual contact," threats that would not justify deadly force in any other location. This effectively creates a broader castle doctrine in which the home is treated as a space warranting heightened defensive rights even against less severe intrusions.

**Initial Aggressor Rule.** Both codes restrict the defense for initial aggressors, but the RCC narrows the definition of provocation. Under subsection (b)(2), the defense is unavailable only when the actor "purposely, through conduct other than speech or presence alone, provokes or brings about the situation" and fails to withdraw. The explicit exclusion of "speech or presence alone" from the category of provocative conduct means that verbal insults, threats, or the mere act of being in a particular location cannot strip a person of the right to self-defense. The MPC contains no comparable carve-out; its initial aggressor limitation turns on whether the actor provoked the encounter without specifying that words alone are insufficient.

**Law Enforcement De-Escalation.** The MPC addresses law enforcement use of force in a separate provision, Section 3.07, which focuses on whether force is immediately necessary to effect an arrest or prevent escape and requires officers to announce their purpose before using force. The RCC takes a markedly different approach by embedding law enforcement standards directly within the self-defense provision and mandating that the factfinder consider specific de-escalation factors: the officer's training and experience, whether the complainant was afforded an opportunity to surrender weapons, whether the officer engaged in de-escalation measures, whether the officer's own conduct increased the risk of a deadly confrontation, and whether the officer made all reasonable efforts to prevent loss of life, including abandoning efforts to apprehend. These factors reflect contemporary police reform principles and give juries structured guidance for evaluating officer-involved shootings, a level of specificity absent from the MPC.

**Enumerated Harms.** The MPC frames self-defense in terms of protection against "unlawful force," a general category. The RCC instead enumerates the specific harms that trigger the defense: physical contact, bodily injury, sexual act, sexual contact, confinement, and death. This specificity clarifies the defense's scope and eliminates ambiguity about what constitutes a sufficient threat, while also expanding coverage to include threats of sexual contact and confinement that do not neatly fit within the MPC's "unlawful force" framework.

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Excuse Defenses {#sec-general-part-excuse}

This section compares the codes' approaches to duress and the insanity defense.

---

### Duress

#### Proposed RCC Provision

RCC Section 22E-501 provides:

> **(a) Affirmative defense.** It is an affirmative defense that, in fact:
>
> (1) The actor reasonably believes:
>
> (A) A person communicated to the actor that the person will cause the actor or a third person a criminal bodily injury, sexual act, sexual contact, confinement, or death; and
>
> (B) The actor or third person is in imminent danger of the communicated harm; and
>
> (2) The communication would cause a reasonable person of the same background and in the same circumstances as the actor to engage in the conduct constituting the offense.
>
> **(b) Exceptions.** This defense is not available when, in fact:
>
> (1) The actor recklessly brings about the situation requiring a choice of harms;
>
> (2) Negligence is the culpable mental state for an objective element of the offense and the actor is negligent in bringing about the situation requiring a choice of harms; or
>
> (3) The conduct constituting the offense is an escape from a correctional facility or officer under RCC Section 22E-3401 and the actor does not make reasonable efforts to safely return to official custody.

#### MPC Counterpart

MPC Section 2.09 provides:

> **(1)** It is an affirmative defense that the actor engaged in the conduct charged to constitute an offense because he was coerced to do so by the use of, or a threat to use, unlawful force against his person or the person of another, that a person of reasonable firmness in his situation would have been unable to resist.
>
> **(2)** The defense provided by this Section is unavailable if the actor recklessly placed himself in a situation in which it was probable that he would be subjected to duress. The defense is also unavailable if he was negligent in placing himself in such a situation, whenever negligence suffices to establish culpability for the offense charged.

#### Key Differences

**Classification and Burden of Proof.** The RCC explicitly designates duress as an "affirmative defense," which under D.C. law means the defendant bears the burden of proving the defense by a preponderance of the evidence. The MPC also labels duress an "affirmative defense," but the practical allocation of burdens varies considerably by jurisdiction: some states require the defendant to prove duress, while others require only that the defendant produce some evidence, after which the prosecution must disprove duress beyond a reasonable doubt. The RCC's unambiguous classification resolves this uncertainty for D.C. courts.

**Imminence Requirement.** The RCC requires that the actor reasonably believe they or a third person is "in imminent danger of the communicated harm." The MPC contains no imminence requirement; it asks only whether a person of reasonable firmness "would have been unable to resist" the threatened unlawful force. This distinction is consequential in cases involving ongoing coercive relationships, such as gang membership or domestic abuse, where threats may be credible and persistent but not immediately executable. Under the MPC, such circumstances can support a duress defense; under the RCC, the defendant must establish that the threatened harm was imminent at the time of the offense.

**Nature of Qualifying Threats.** The two codes differ substantially in the types of threats that activate the defense. The MPC requires only "unlawful force against his person or the person of another," a broad category encompassing any physical coercion that is not legally authorized. The RCC limits qualifying threats to specific enumerated harms: "criminal bodily injury, sexual act, sexual contact, confinement, or death." This narrower formulation excludes threats of property destruction, economic ruin, or reputational harm, which might constitute "unlawful force" in some interpretations of the MPC. The RCC's enumerated approach ensures that only threats of serious personal harm can excuse criminal conduct.

**Reasonable Person Standard.** Both codes incorporate an objective component, but they frame it differently. The MPC asks whether "a person of reasonable firmness in his situation" would have been unable to resist the coercion. The RCC asks whether "a reasonable person of the same background and in the same circumstances as the actor" would have engaged in the criminal conduct. The RCC's formulation is arguably more individualized, inviting consideration of the actor's specific background, which could encompass factors such as age, prior experiences of violence, or cultural context, that bear on how a reasonable person in the defendant's position would have responded to the threat.

**Fault in Creating the Situation.** Both provisions deny the defense when the actor was culpably responsible for creating the coercive situation, and both calibrate the exclusion to the culpability requirement of the charged offense. The RCC bars the defense when the actor "recklessly brings about the situation requiring a choice of harms," and extends the bar to negligent creation when negligence suffices for the offense. The MPC adopts essentially the same structure. This parallel reflects a shared recognition that a person who voluntarily enters a situation likely to produce coercive pressure, such as joining a criminal enterprise, should not later invoke that pressure as an excuse.

**Prison Escape.** The RCC includes a specific exception for escape from a correctional facility: the defense is unavailable unless the actor "make[s] reasonable efforts to safely return to official custody." This codifies a practical concern that the MPC does not expressly address. Under the MPC, the general duress framework would apply to prison escape without any specific obligation to surrender, leaving courts to work out the limits of the defense case by case.

---

### Mental Disability Defense (Insanity)

#### Proposed RCC Provision

RCC Section 22E-504 provides:

> **(a) Affirmative defense.** It is an affirmative defense in a criminal proceeding that, in fact, as a result of a mental disability, the actor:
>
> (1) Lacked substantial capacity to conform their conduct to the requirements of the law; or
>
> (2) Lacked substantial capacity to recognize the wrongfulness of their conduct.
>
> **(b) Effect of defense.** An actor who is acquitted solely on the ground of mental disability shall be committed under D.C. Code Section 24-501.
>
> **(c) Definitions.**
>
> ...
>
> (2) In this section, the term "mental disability" means an abnormal condition of the mind, regardless of its medical label, that affects mental or emotional processes and either substantially impairs a person's ability to regulate and control their conduct or substantially impairs a person's ability to recognize the wrongfulness of their conduct.
>
> **(d) Interpretation of statute.** This section shall not be construed to create or limit a court's authority, on its own initiative, to order a psychiatric examination or to raise a mental disability defense.

#### MPC Counterpart

MPC Section 4.01 provides:

> **(1)** A person is not responsible for criminal conduct if at the time of such conduct as a result of mental disease or defect he lacks substantial capacity either to appreciate the criminality [wrongfulness] of his conduct or to conform his conduct to the requirements of law.
>
> **(2)** As used in this Section, the terms "mental disease or defect" do not include an abnormality manifested only by repeated criminal or otherwise antisocial conduct.

#### Key Differences

**Substantive Standard: Essential Continuity.** The RCC and MPC adopt virtually identical substantive tests for the insanity defense, both rooted in the ALI (American Law Institute) formulation. Each requires that, as a result of a mental condition, the actor either (1) lacked substantial capacity to conform their conduct to the requirements of law, or (2) lacked substantial capacity to appreciate or recognize the wrongfulness of their conduct. The RCC's decision to retain this standard is notable because many jurisdictions abandoned the volitional prong (the capacity-to-conform test) after John Hinckley's acquittal in 1982, adopting cognitive-only tests. By preserving both prongs, the RCC maintains the position that a person whose mental condition renders them unable to control their behavior, even if they understand it is wrong, should not be held criminally responsible.

**Terminology: "Mental Disability" vs. "Mental Disease or Defect."** The most visible difference is terminological. The MPC uses the traditional phrase "mental disease or defect," which carries clinical connotations and has generated extensive litigation over what qualifies as a "disease" in the legal sense. The RCC replaces this with "mental disability," defined as "an abnormal condition of the mind, regardless of its medical label, that affects mental or emotional processes." The phrase "regardless of its medical label" deliberately decouples the legal standard from diagnostic categories, signaling that the inquiry is functional rather than nosological. What matters is the condition's effect on the actor's capacity, not whether psychiatrists classify it under a particular heading in the DSM.

**Exclusion of Psychopathy and Antisocial Conduct.** The MPC explicitly excludes from "mental disease or defect" any "abnormality manifested only by repeated criminal or otherwise antisocial conduct." This provision targets psychopathy and antisocial personality disorder, preventing defendants from bootstrapping a pattern of criminal behavior into an insanity defense. The RCC does not contain an identical exclusion, but its definition of "mental disability" achieves a comparable result through different means: by requiring an "abnormal condition of the mind" that "affects mental or emotional processes," the RCC implicitly excludes conditions whose only manifestation is behavioral rather than cognitive or emotional. A defendant whose sole claim is that they have a history of antisocial conduct, without evidence of an underlying condition affecting mental or emotional processes, would not satisfy the RCC's definition.

**Mandatory Commitment.** Both codes provide for post-acquittal commitment, but they do so with different degrees of specificity. The RCC states flatly that an actor "acquitted solely on the ground of mental disability shall be committed under D.C. Code Section 24-501," incorporating the existing D.C. statutory framework for commitment following an insanity acquittal. The MPC addresses commitment in Section 4.08, which provides for automatic commitment and subsequent release procedures. The practical effect is similar: in both systems, a successful insanity defense does not result in unconditional freedom but rather in mandatory civil commitment, addressing the public safety concerns that have historically driven opposition to the insanity defense.

**Judicial Authority.** The RCC includes a provision absent from the MPC: subsection (d) clarifies that the statute "shall not be construed to create or limit a court's authority, on its own initiative, to order a psychiatric examination or to raise a mental disability defense." This preserves the court's independent ability to inject the insanity question into proceedings even when neither party raises it, a safeguard for defendants whose mental condition may prevent them from recognizing the relevance of the defense. The MPC contains no analogous provision, leaving the scope of judicial initiative to be determined by procedural rules and case law.

**"Recognize" vs. "Appreciate."** A subtle textual difference appears in the cognitive prong. The MPC asks whether the actor lacked substantial capacity to "appreciate" the criminality or wrongfulness of their conduct, a term chosen by the ALI drafters to convey a deeper, more emotionally integrated understanding than mere intellectual awareness. The RCC uses "recognize," which may carry a somewhat narrower connotation, suggesting awareness or acknowledgment rather than the fuller emotional comprehension implied by "appreciate." Whether this difference proves consequential in practice will depend on how D.C. courts interpret the term, but it represents a potential narrowing of the cognitive inquiry.

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Additional Resources {#sec-general-part-resources}

For further reading on the topics covered in this appendix:

- **CCRC Final Report and Commentary.** The Criminal Code Reform Commission's [final recommendations](https://ccrc.dc.gov/page/recommendations){.cite} include detailed commentary on each General Part provision, explaining the drafters' reasoning and comparing their choices to the MPC, current D.C. law, and approaches in other jurisdictions.

- **Model Penal Code (1962).** The American Law Institute's [Model Penal Code and Commentaries](https://www.ali.org/publications/model-penal-code){.cite} remain the foundational reference for understanding the MPC's structure and the policy choices it embodies.

- **Robinson & Grall, "Element Analysis in Defining Criminal Liability."** Paul Robinson and Jane Grall's influential article in the Stanford Law Review provides a comprehensive account of the element-analysis framework that both the MPC and RCC employ.

- **Dubber, "An Introduction to the Model Penal Code."** Markus Dubber's guide offers an accessible overview of the MPC's General Part provisions, useful for students encountering the element-analysis framework for the first time.
